content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Somewhere in the last three years, AI-powered became a label that could be attached to almost anything and mean almost nothing. Almost every enterprise product now carries it. Very few of them have changed how the business actually runs.
That gap — between the label and the result — is the subject of a new piece from our CEO, Irfan Khan, published on Forbes Technology Council.
The argument, in one line: stop grading AI by the demo. Grade it by the operator.
The operator is not impressed
Every large enterprise has, somewhere in the building, a pragmatic line-of-business leader with a quota, a deadline, and a regulator to answer to. A CFO who needs the close to actually shorten. A supply chain leader who needs the planning cycle to actually compress. A claims executive who needs the loss ratio to actually move.
Those are the operators Irfan writes about. They are not going to be moved by another beautiful standalone demo. They are going to be moved — or not — by whether the AI program produced a result they can point to on a Tuesday afternoon.
If it did not, in his framing, the program is not real yet. It is a hobby.
Where CLOUDSUFI is spending its energy
The constraint on real AI outcomes in 2026, Irfan argues, is not the model. The models are already extraordinary. The constraint is the wiring — the messy, unglamorous, deeply organizational work of connecting AI to the systems where business actually happens. The ERP. The core banking platform. The data warehouse no one wants to touch. The Excel file the team has quietly been running on for fifteen years.
That is where the value lives. It is also where most AI initiatives have refused to go, because it is harder to demo than a chatbot.
It is also, not coincidentally, where CLOUDSUFI spends most of our energy. Data-driven digital transformation is not a slogan for us — it is a description of the seam between AI and the real systems of the enterprise, and it is the seam we help our customers close.
The rest of the argument
Irfan’s Forbes piece goes deeper — the three convictions we are planting as a company for the year, why the demo era failed the people who actually run the business, and what he is asking every CEO to do about it before the next budget cycle.
Read the full piece on Forbes → https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbestechcouncil/2026/06/12/the-rise-of-the-operator-when-ai-stops-being-a-demo/
One thing worth doing this week, regardless of where you are on your AI journey: walk down the hall to your most pragmatic operator. Ask them, in their language, what changed for you this quarter because of AI.
The answer will tell you where the second half of 2026 needs to go.
